content.text: Provisioned throughput — or token capacity commitments — are commercial arrangements where an organisation pays a fixed monthly fee in exchange for guaranteed token capacity at a discounted per-token rate. The economics are straightforward: pay a premium if the workload is unpredictable; commit to predictable capacity and save significantly. The challenge is that the commitment is a financial obligation regardless of whether the capacity is used — underutilised commitments are money burned with no business value delivered.

attrs.alt: Token capacity commitment vs on-demand trade-off
attrs.title: Cost vs. risk comparison for on-demand and committed throughput

content.text: How provider capacity commitment models work

Each major AI provider structures provisioned throughput commitments differently, but the core mechanics are similar: you pre-purchase tokens or throughput capacity for a fixed term (typically 1 month to 1 year) and receive a per-token discount in return.
Provider
Commitment unit
Typical term
Typical discount
Rollover policy
Anthropic (Claude)
Tokens per month or throughput units
Monthly or annual
No rollover — unused tokens expire
OpenAI
Token credits or throughput
Monthly
Up to 30%
No rollover
Google (Gemini)
Character/token quota packs
Monthly
Variable by tier
No rollover
Azure OpenAI
Provisioned throughput units (PTUs)
Monthly or annual
No rollover; minimum purchase applies

The no-rollover policy is a critical detail: tokens committed but not consumed by the end of the period are forfeited. This makes utilisation tracking the most important FinOps discipline for commitment management.
Break-even utilisation analysis

A commitment only saves money if utilisation is high enough. The break-even utilisation is the fraction of committed capacity that must be consumed for the commitment to cost the same as on-demand would have. Above this threshold, the commitment is cheaper; below it, the commitment is more expensive.

For most workloads the break-even sits between 70–80% utilisation. Committing to capacity for a workload that is expected to average 85% utilisation with a 10% standard deviation is a sound financial decision — there is a high probability of coming in above break-even. Committing for a workload at 60% average utilisation with high variance is risky.
Commitment sizing methodology

Sizing a commitment requires projecting the workload's monthly token consumption with enough confidence to choose a commitment level. The sizing process uses the forecast models established in the cost forecasting sub-topic, but applies more conservative assumptions because commitment errors are financially consequential.
Compute the rolling 3-month average token consumption for the workload at the model level being committed.
Apply the compound growth model to project consumption for the commitment period (typically the next 3–12 months).
Choose the P50 projected consumption as the commitment size — not the P80. The P80 budget is for protecting against overspend; the P50 commitment size is for optimising cost.
Verify that the expected average utilisation of the commitment exceeds 80% (comfortable above break-even).
If the projection has high variance (stdev > 20% of mean), reduce the commitment size to the P40 or P30 projected consumption and cover the upper tail with on-demand.
Hybrid on-demand and committed architecture

The optimal cost structure for most large AI programmes is a hybrid: committed capacity covers the predictable base load, and on-demand covers variable peaks. The split point is the level of consumption that the organisation is 95% confident it will reach in any given month.

import statistics

def optimal_commitment_size(
    monthly_token_history: list[float],   # historical monthly consumption in millions
    confidence_pct: float = 0.90,         # target % of months where usage >= commitment
    commitment_discount_pct: float = 0.30,
) -> dict:
    """
    Compute the optimal commitment size such that actual usage meets or exceeds
    the commitment in at least confidence_pct of months.
    
    Returns the commitment size and expected annual savings vs. all-on-demand.
    """
    sorted_history = sorted(monthly_token_history)
    # The commitment size should be at the (1 - confidence_pct) percentile
    # so that usage >= commitment in confidence_pct fraction of months
    idx = int((1 - confidence_pct) * len(sorted_history))
    commitment_1m = sorted_history[max(idx, 0)]

    avg_usage = statistics.mean(monthly_token_history)
    on_demand_rate = 3.00  # $/1M (example)
    committed_rate = on_demand_rate * (1 - commitment_discount_pct)

    monthly_on_demand_cost = avg_usage * on_demand_rate
    # Hybrid cost: committed portion at committed rate, remainder at on-demand
    monthly_hybrid_cost = (commitment_1m * committed_rate
                           + max(avg_usage - commitment_1m, 0) * on_demand_rate)
    monthly_saving = monthly_on_demand_cost - monthly_hybrid_cost

    return {
        "optimal_commitment_1m": round(commitment_1m, 0),
        "expected_utilisation_pct": round(commitment_1m / avg_usage * 100, 1),
        "monthly_saving_usd": round(monthly_saving, 2),
        "annual_saving_usd": round(monthly_saving * 12, 2),
    }

history = [850, 920, 1050, 980, 1100, 1020, 970, 1080, 1010, 950, 1030, 1060]
print(optimal_commitment_size(history))
Commitment structures: tokens vs. throughput units

Some providers offer commitments in units of throughput (requests per minute or tokens per minute) rather than total monthly tokens. These are fundamentally different financial instruments and require different analysis.
Commitment type
What you purchase
Risk if underused
Risk if overused
Total monthly tokens
Token bucket consumed over the month
Tokens forfeited at month end
Pay on-demand for excess tokens
Tokens per minute (throughput)
Rate capacity, not volume
Capacity idle if usage is below rate
Requests queued or rate-limited
Throughput units (PTUs)
Equivalent to tokens/minute capacity
Fixed cost regardless of actual volume
Requests rejected at capacity

For workloads with bursty traffic (high peak-to-average ratio), throughput unit commitments can be more expensive than monthly token commitments even at the same discount rate because throughput must be sized for the peak, not the average.
Governance: who approves commitments

A token capacity commitment is a multi-month financial obligation. It should be approved through the same process as other forward-looking financial commitments, not treated as a routine procurement decision.
Commitments < $5,000/month: FinOps lead approval with finance partner notification.
Commitments $5,000–$25,000/month: FinOps lead + finance partner joint approval.
Commitments > $25,000/month or > 6-month term: VP Engineering or CFO approval; included in the annual budget plan.
Any commitment before a workload has 3 months of production history: requires an explicit forecast signed off by the product owner and finance partner.
Commitment renewal and renegotiation cadence

Token capacity commitments typically renew automatically unless cancelled before a deadline (often 30–60 days before the renewal date). The FinOps team must maintain a commitment calendar to avoid inadvertent renewals of commitments for workloads whose usage has changed.
Time before renewal
Action
90 days
Review utilisation for the past 3 months; flag commitments below 75% utilisation for potential reduction
60 days
Decide: renew at same level, resize, or cancel; initiate any needed provider negotiations
30 days
Submit renewal decision to provider; confirm discount terms for next term
Day of renewal
Verify new commitment is active in the provider portal; update the commitment registry
Pooling commitments across teams

A single organisation-level commitment can be more efficient than per-team commitments when the teams' usage patterns are not perfectly correlated. If team A peaks in the first half of the month and team B in the second half, a shared commitment sized to their combined average is better utilised than two separate commitments each sized to their individual peaks.
Aggregate model-level usage across all teams to identify the organisation's total token consumption pattern for each model.
Size the organisation-level commitment to the P50 of the aggregate monthly consumption for that model.

Internally allocate the committed tokens to teams using a priority queue: teams with time-sensitive production workloads get first call on the committed capacity; best-effort batch workloads use any remaining capacity before on-demand overflow.
Report commitment utilisation at the organisation level, not per team — teams do not 'own' a share of the commitment, they draw from a shared pool.
Tracking committed vs. on-demand spend

The finance team needs visibility into how much of total AI spend is covered by committed rates vs. on-demand rates. This split matters for budget forecasting (committed spend is more predictable) and for negotiation leverage (a high committed fraction demonstrates volume intent to providers).

A high on-demand fraction (> 50% of spend at on-demand rates) when total volume is large is a strong signal that the organisation should negotiate a larger commitment or explore volume discount tiers, rather than continuing to pay list price for predictable workloads.
Multi-model commitment portfolio management

An enterprise running multiple AI models (Claude Sonnet, Claude Haiku, GPT-4o, Gemini Pro) has a portfolio of commitments across providers and model tiers. The portfolio must be managed as a whole, with the FinOps team tracking the aggregate committed spend, utilisation, and renewal dates in a single commitment registry.
Commitment registry: a spreadsheet or database table recording model ID, provider, monthly committed tokens, monthly fee, effective date, end date, auto-renewal status, and current utilisation.
Renewal dashboard: a calendar view of commitment renewal dates with 90/60/30-day warning flags; reviewed in the monthly FinOps meeting.

Portfolio rebalancing: when a new model tier is adopted (e.g., a team migrates from Sonnet to Haiku for cost reduction), the Sonnet commitment may become over-sized and the Haiku commitment under-sized; rebalancing is an annual optimisation opportunity.

Cross-provider consolidation: if the organisation uses both Anthropic and OpenAI for similar workloads, evaluate whether consolidating volume with one provider unlocks a larger discount tier than splitting across both.
Tax and accounting treatment of commitments

From an accounting perspective, a prepaid or committed AI usage arrangement may need to be treated differently than a pay-as-you-go service. Finance teams with GAAP or IFRS obligations should understand the recognition implications.
Monthly commitment fees are typically recognised as an operating expense in the month they are incurred — no capitalisation required for straightforward software-as-a-service arrangements.
Annual prepaid commitments (where the full year's fee is paid upfront) should be recognised as a prepaid expense and amortised monthly over the commitment term.
Unused committed capacity that cannot be rolled over has no asset value at period end — write down any remaining prepaid balance associated with forfeited capacity.
Consult your accounting team before signing a multi-year commitment if the contract includes minimum usage guarantees, as these may trigger variable consideration treatment under ASC 606 or IFRS 15.
Communicating commitment decisions to engineering teams

Engineering teams need to know which model capacity is covered by a commitment and which requests may fall through to on-demand pricing. Without this information, teams cannot make informed decisions about when to run batch workloads to maximise committed capacity utilisation.
Publish the current commitment portfolio in the developer portal: model ID, committed tokens remaining this month, estimated days until exhaustion at current burn rate.
Alert engineering leads when committed capacity is 80% consumed and estimated to exhaust before month end — giving them the option to shift non-urgent batch work to the next month.
Notify teams when their workload is being served from on-demand capacity rather than committed capacity (if the provider exposes this distinction in the usage API).
Include committed vs. on-demand spend in the weekly digest: 'Your team used 950M tokens this week; 820M (86%) from committed capacity; 130M (14%) on-demand.'
Common mistakes

Committing before the workload has a stable usage pattern: the first 2–3 months of a new workload are not representative — the usage profile changes rapidly as the feature is adopted and prompt-optimised.

Sizing the commitment to the average without accounting for variance: a workload averaging 1B tokens/month with 30% monthly variance will be below commitment in many months; size to a lower percentile.
Forgetting the no-rollover policy: teams that plan to 'catch up' on committed tokens in the last week of the month routinely fail to do so — unused tokens are money lost.
Treating throughput and token commitments as interchangeable: they serve different purposes and have different risk profiles.
Not reviewing commitment utilisation monthly: a commitment that was right-sized at purchase can become underutilised as workloads change — monthly utilisation review is mandatory.
Key takeaways
Commitments offer 15–40% discounts but carry underutilisation risk — unused tokens are forfeited with no rollover.
Break-even utilisation typically sits at 70–80%; commit only for workloads expected to sustain usage above this threshold.
Size commitments at the P40–P50 projected consumption and cover the upper tail on-demand — this optimises cost without over-committing.
Use a hybrid architecture: committed base load at discounted rates, on-demand for variable peaks.
Treat commitments as financial obligations requiring approval at the appropriate level — not routine procurement decisions.
